blurb: A Plant Select workhorse — one of Bruce Meyers' Mexicali crosses of showy Mexican penstemons with hardy American kinds, throwing dense wands of violet-purple flowers all summer over a tidy mound of narrow dark-green leaves.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
seasons: Violet-purple flower wands June–August; tidy mound of narrow dark-green leaves; tan seed stalks into fall
wildlife: Bees and other pollinators; long summer bloom feeds native bees
gaps: Photos are species-representative of the Mexicali parent species Penstemon campanulatus (violet-purple tubular flowers, narrow dark-green leaves — the same look as 'Pikes Peak Purple'), honestly captioned: this named ×mexicali hybrid has no clean iNaturalist taxon and no open-licensed cultivar-specific photo set exists. No clean (non-hand-held) open-licensed seed-capsule shot of the parent species was available, so the reel covers flower/bloom-mass/habit/foliage/context instead of a dedicated seedhead.

care.hardiness: Cold-hardy across the Front Range — rated USDA zones 4b–8 (hardy to about −20°F), comfortably within Boulder's 5b–6a and good to roughly 6,200 ft; longer-lived than many border penstemons but still appreciates the lean, dry conditions that keep it going.[1]
care.planting: Plant out in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, into lean fast-draining ground in full sun; don't amend with compost. In containers use a gritty, sharply draining mix and keep the crown dry — potted penstemon crowns rot far more easily than plants in open ground.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun to part sun — at least six hours — for upright, well-colored violet spikes; too much shade makes it floppy and shy-flowering.[1][2]
care.soil: Adaptable to clay, loam or sandy soil and even disturbed ground, but it must drain sharply; it resents soggy, rich soil. Plant in a rock garden, on a slope, or in a gritty bed rather than amending heavy clay.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate, xeric once established; give it spring moisture to bloom, then back off — overwatering, especially after flowering, brings on crown rot.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 12–15 in apart; the mound reaches roughly 14–18 in tall in bloom and 12–14 in wide.[1]
care.propagation: Being a sterile-ish named hybrid it's usually increased vegetatively: take basal or softwood cuttings (penstemons resent division, so don't split the crown). Any seed it sets will not come true to the named clone but can be cold-moist stratified and grown on for new plants.[1][2]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify any saved seed (fall sowing supplies the natural winter chilling) to break dormancy; the named hybrid itself is best cloned by cuttings.[2]
care.bloom: Dense wands of violet-purple tubular flowers from June into August, often blooming sporadically on through late summer; deadhead spent spikes to neaten and prolong the display.[1]
care.feeding: No fertilizer needed in normal soil — feeding makes floppy growth and shortens its life.[2]
care.maintenance: Low: deadhead spent spikes, then ease off summer water to prevent crown rot; tidy old flower stems in late fall or early spring.[1][2]
care.troubles: No serious pests or diseases; root and crown rot strike in wet, poorly drained or over-amended soil, and rust, grasshoppers and flea beetles are occasional.[2]
care.companions: A dependable mid-border and rock-garden plant for hot, dry sites — pairs with other xeric penstemons, salvias, agastache, blanketflower and grasses.[1]

references.name: Plant Select — Pikes Peak Purple® penstemon (Penstemon ×mexicali 'P007S')

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally for its long violet-purple bloom — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[2]
